source_title: From the Transistor to the Web Browser
policy: The original 24 bullets are preserved verbatim here. Catalog modules may narrow or adapt implementation choices, but each source item maps exactly once.

sections.original_heading: Section 1: Intro: Cheating our way past the transistor
Section 2: Bringup: What language is hardware coded in?
Section 3: Processor: What is a processor anyway?
Section 5: Operating System: Software we take for granted
Section 7: Physical: Running on real hardware

items.title: So about those transistors
items.original_text: So about those transistors -- Course overview. Describe how FPGAs are buildable using transistors, and that ICs are just collections of transistors in a nice reliable package. Understand the LUTs and stuff. Talk briefly about the theory of transistors, but all projects must build on each other so we can’t build one.

items.title: Emulation
items.original_text: Emulation -- Building on real hardware limits the reach of this course. Using something like Verilator will allow anyone with a computer to play.

items.title: Blinking an LED
items.original_text: Blinking an LED(Verilog, 10) -- Your first little program! Getting the simulator working. Learning Verilog.

items.title: Building a UART
items.original_text: Building a UART(Verilog, 100) -- An intro chapter to Verilog, copy a real UART, introducing the concept of MMIO, though the serial port may be semihosting. Serial test echo program and led control.

items.title: Coding an assembler
items.original_text: Coding an assembler(Python, 500) -- Straightforward and boring, write in python. Happens in parallel with the CPU building. Teaches you ARM assembly. Initially outputs just binary files, but changed when you write a linker.

items.title: Building a ARM7 CPU
items.original_text: Building a ARM7 CPU(Verilog, 1500) -- Break this into subchapters. A simple pipeline to start, decode, fetch, execute. How much BRAM do we have? We need at least 1MB, DDR would be hard I think, maybe an SRAM. Simulatable and synthesizable.

items.title: Coding a bootrom
items.original_text: Coding a bootrom(Assembler, 40) -- This allows code download into RAM over the serial port, and is baked into the FPGA image. Cute test programs run on this.

items.title: Building a C compiler
items.original_text: Building a C compiler(Haskell, 2000) -- A bit more interesting, cover the basics of compiler design. Write in haskell. Write a parser. Break this into subchapters. Outputs ARM assembly.

items.title: Building a linker
items.original_text: Building a linker(Python, 300) -- If you are clever, this should take a day. Output elf files. Use for testing with QEMU, semihosting.

items.title: libc + malloc
items.original_text: libc + malloc(C, 500) -- The gateway to more complicated programs. libc is only half here, things like memcpy and memset and printf, but no syscall wrappers.

items.title: Building an ethernet controller
items.original_text: Building an ethernet controller(Verilog, 200) -- Talk to a real PHY, consider carefully MMIO design.

items.title: Writing a bootloader
items.original_text: Writing a bootloader(C, 300) -- Write ethernet program to boot kernel over UDP. First thing written in C. Maybe don’t redownload over serial each time and embed in FPGA image.

items.title: Building an MMU
items.original_text: Building an MMU(Verilog, 1000) -- ARM9ish, explain TLBs and other fun things. Maybe also a memory controller, depending on how the FPGA is, then add the init code to your bootloader.

items.title: Building an operating system
items.original_text: Building an operating system(C, 2500) -- UNIXish, only user space threads. (open, read, write, close), (fork, execve, wait, sleep, exit), (mmap, munmap, mprotect). Consider the debug interface you are using, ranging from printf to perhaps a gdbremote stub into kernel. Break into subchapters.

items.title: Talking to an SD card
items.original_text: Talking to an SD card(Verilog, 150) -- The last hardware you have to do. And a driver

items.title: FAT
items.original_text: FAT(C, 300) -- A real filesystem, I think fat is the simplest

items.title: init, shell, download, cat, ls, rm
items.original_text: init, shell, download, cat, ls, rm(C, 250) -- Your first user space programs.

items.title: Building a TCP stack
items.original_text: Building a TCP stack(C, 500) -- Probably coded in the kernel, integrate the ethernet driver into the kernel. Add support for networking syscalls to kernel. (send, recv, bind, connect)

items.title: telnetd, the power of being multiprocess
items.original_text: telnetd, the power of being multiprocess(C, 50) --  Written in C, user can connect multiple times with telnet. Really just a bind shell.

items.title: Space saving dynamic linking
items.original_text: Space saving dynamic linking(C, 300) -- Because we can, explain how dynamic linker is just a user space program. Changes to linker required.

items.title: So about that web
items.original_text: So about that web(C, 500+) -- A “nice” text based web browser, using ANSI and terminal niceness. Dynamically linked and nice, nice as you want.

items.title: Talking to an FPGA
items.original_text: Talking to an FPGA(C, 200) -- A little code for the USB MCU to bitbang JTAG.

items.title: Building an FPGA board
items.original_text: Building an FPGA board -- Board design, FPGA BGA reflow, FPGA flash, a 50mhz clock, a USB JTAG port and flasher(no special hardware, a little cypress usb mcu to do jtag), a few leds, a reset button, a serial port(USB-FTDI) also powering via USB, an sd card, expansion connector(ide cable?), and an ethernet port. Optional, expansion board, host USB port, NTSC TV out, an ISA port, and PS/2 connector on the board to taunt you. We provide a toaster oven and a multimeter thermometer to do reflow.

items.title: Bringup
items.original_text: Bringup -- Compiling and downloading the Verilog for the board
